messages.content:
Good evening.
Today, our fellow citizens, our way of life, our very freedom came under attack in a series of deliberate and deadly terrorist acts. The victims were in airplanes or in their offices: secretaries, business men and women, military and federal workers, moms and dads, friends and neighbors.
Thousands of lives were suddenly ended by evil, despicable acts of terror. The pictures of airplanes flying into buildings, fires burning, huge - huge structures collapsing have filled us with disbelief, terrible sadness, and a quiet, unyielding anger. These acts of mass murder were intended to frighten our nation into chaos and retreat. But they have failed. Our country is strong.
A great people has been moved to defend a great nation. Terrorist attacks can shake the foundations of our biggest buildings, but they cannot touch the foundation of America. These acts shatter steel, but they cannot dent the steel of American resolve. America was targeted for attack because we're the brightest beacon for freedom and opportunity in the world. And no one will keep that light from shining. Today, our nation saw evil - the very worst of human nature - and we responded with the best of America. With the daring of our rescue workers, with the caring for strangers and neighbors who came to give blood and help in any way they could.
Immediately following the first attack, I implemented our government's emergency response plans. Our military is powerful, and it's prepared.
Our emergency teams are working in New York City and Washington D.C. to help with local rescue efforts. Our first priority is to get help to those who have been injured, and to take every precaution to protect our citizens at home and around the world from further attacks. The functions of our government continue without interruption. Federal agencies in Washington which had to be evacuated today are reopening for business as well
The search is underway for those who were behind these evil acts. I have directed the full resources of our intelligence and law enforcement communities to find those responsible and to bring them to justice. We will make no distinction between the terrorists who committed these acts and those who harbor them.
1 appreciate so very much the members of Congres who have joined me in strongly condemning these attacks. And on behalf of the American people, I thank the many world leaders who have called to offer their condolences and assistance. America and our friends and allies join with all those who want peace and security in the world, and we stand together to win the war against terrorism.
Tonight, I ask for your prayers for all those who grieve, for the children whose worlds have been shattered, for all whose sense of safety and security has been threatened. And I pray they will be comforted by a Power greater than any of us, spoken through the ages in Psalm 23:
Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I fear no evil for you are with me.
This is a day when all Americans from every walk of life unite in our resolve for justice and peace. America has stood down enemies before, and we will do so this time. None of us will ever forget this day, yet we go forward to defend freedom and all that is good and just in our world.
Thank you, and God bless America.
How does President Bush characterize the larger or more symbolic significance of the 9/11 attacks in his speech? Explain.

President Bush characterizes the 9/11 attacks as a deliberate and deadly terrorist act intended to attack not only American citizens, but also America's way of life, freedom, and democracy. He describes the victims of the attacks as ordinary people from all walks of life, highlighting the indiscriminate and senseless nature of the violence. 

Furthermore, President Bush characterizes the attacks as a reflection of the "very worst of human nature" and an assault on the values that America represents, including freedom, opportunity, and democracy. He emphasizes that the attackers targeted America precisely because it represents a beacon of hope and a model for others to emulate. 

Despite the scale and horror of the attacks, President Bush emphasizes that they will not succeed in shaking America's resolve or denting its sense of national unity. He highlights the bravery and generosity of ordinary citizens who stepped forward to help in the aftermath of the attacks, demonstrating the best of American values and character.

Overall, President Bush characterizes the 9/11 attacks as an unprecedented and monstrous assault on America's way of life, democracy, and values, but one that ultimately strengthened America's resolve to defend freedom, justice, and peace in the world.
resúmelo

In his speech, President Bush characterizes the 9/11 attacks as a deliberate and heinous terrorist act that targeted America's way of life, freedom, and democracy. He highlights the victims as ordinary people from all walks of life and emphasizes that the attacks represent the worst of human nature and an assault on America's values. Despite the scale of the attacks, President Bush highlights America's strength and unity, with ordinary citizens stepping forward to help in the aftermath. Overall, the attacks strengthened America's resolve to defend freedom, justice, and peace in the world.
mas resumido

President Bush says that the 9/11 attacks were terrible and that they targeted America's people, values, and freedom. Despite this, America is strong, and the attacks made America more determined to defend freedom and justice.
What distinction does Bush claim will not be made in the pursuit of justice for the attacks?
President Bush claims that no distinction will be made between the terrorists who carried out the 9/11 attacks and those who harbor and support them in the pursuit of justice.
Why does President Bush quote this particular Biblical passage from Psalm 23 near the end of his address? Explain how he ties it to the day's events.

President Bush quotes Psalm 23 in order to offer consolation to the American people affected by the 9/11 attacks. The passage is meant to provide comfort and hope to those grieving and struggling to come to terms with the tragedy. 

He ties the passage to the day's events by describing the 9/11 attacks as a "valley of the shadow of death" that the American people are currently walking through. The passage reminds them that they need not fear for "you" (a reference to God) are with them.

In quoting Psalm 23, President Bush is appealing to a shared spiritual and moral tradition while highlighting the importance of faith in trying times. The remembrance and recognition of God's guidance and support can provide a source of comfort and strength as the country moves forward from the devastating attacks.
resumelo

President Bush quotes Psalm 23 in order to provide comfort and hope to those affected by the 9/11 attacks. The passage reminds them that they need not fear because God is with them in this "valley of the shadow of death" caused by the attacks. By quoting the Psalm, President Bush highlights the importance of faith and God's guidance during trying times.
Use context clues from the speech to determine the meaning of chaos as it is used in paragraph 2. Write your best definition of the word chaos here, explaining how you figured it out.

In paragraph 2, President Bush says, "These acts of mass murder were intended to frighten our nation into chaos and retreat." Here, chaos refers to a state of confusion, panic, and disorder. 

This meaning can be inferred from the preceding sentence where President Bush describes the attacks as deliberate and intended to cause fear, and by the following sentence which emphasizes America's strength in the face of the attacks. The phrase "chaos and retreat" suggests that the terrorists wanted to sow panic and confusion in American society to undermine its values and institutions.

Read the following dictionary entry:
resolve
re•solve /razälv/ noun
1. willpower or determination
verb
1. to settle or bring a truce
2. to decide on a course of action
Use context to determine which of these definitions most closely matches the use of resolve in the speech. Write the best definition of resoive here and explain how you figured it out.

In the speech, President Bush uses the word "resolve" to mean willpower or determination. 

He uses the word to describe the American people's unwavering spirit and their determination to defend freedom and justice in the face of the attacks. For example, he says, "These acts shatter steel but they cannot dent the steel of American resolve." Here, resolve refers to the unyielding determination of the American people to stand together and defend their values against those who seek to undermine them.

This meaning of resolve as willpower or determination is reflected in the first definition provided in the dictionary entry.
resumelo

In the speech, President Bush uses the word "resolve" to mean willpower or determination. The American people's resolve refers to their unyielding spirit and determination to defend freedom and justice against those seeking to undermine them. This meaning of resolve corresponds to the first definition provided in the dictionary entry.

Consider the tone of President Bush's address to the nation following the 9/11 attacks. What do you think the president is trying to accomplish in this address? What message(s) do you think he is trying to convey as head of state? Explain his approach to this momentous, tragic day and how he characterizes the symbolic meaning of these attacks against the United States.

The tone of President Bush's address is solemn, resolute, and patriotic. He is attempting to reassure and comfort the American people in the aftermath of the 9/11 attacks, while also conveying a strong message of determination and resolve.

As head of state, President Bush is emphasizing the importance of national unity, resilience, and strength in responding to the attacks. His approach is to inspire a sense of shared purpose and solidarity among all Americans, regardless of their background or beliefs.

President Bush characterizes the symbolic meaning of the attacks as an assault on America's values, freedom, and democracy. He acknowledges the enormity of the tragedy and the sense of disbelief and anger it has caused, while also highlighting the bravery and selflessness of those who responded to the crisis.

In addition, he emphasizes the importance of pursuing justice and bringing those responsible for the attacks to account. He vows that no distinction will be made between the terrorists who committed the acts and those who harbor or support them, indicating America's resolve to confront and defeat terrorism.

Overall, President Bush's address reflects a sense of national mourning and loss, but also a strong commitment to defending America's values, democracy, and freedom.
resume la respuesta en un texto corto

In his address, President Bush aims to reassure and comfort Americans after the 9/11 attacks while emphasizing the importance of national unity, strength, and resilience. He characterizes the attacks as an assault on America's values and democracy, highlighting the bravery of those who responded to the crisis. Additionally, he vows to pursue justice and bring the attackers to account, indicating America's commitment to defeating terrorism. Overall, his address reflects a sense of mourning but also a strong resolve to defend America's values and freedom.
mas resumido

President Bush's address aims to comfort and reassure Americans after the 9/11 attacks and emphasize the importance of national unity and resilience. He characterizes the attacks as an assault on America's values, and vows to pursue justice and defeat terrorism.

Complete the chart by dragging and dropping the correct meaning into the third column to match the term in each row.
Meaning Options (6 of 6)
information and evidence used to prove or corroborate sgmething il implied but not stated directly

the most important point that an author makes about a topic in a text
I to examine the parts of something in detail
precisely and clearly expressed, leaving no room for doubt il the subject of a text, usually expressed as a single word or phrase in the form of a noun Term
analyze
central idea
explicit

| Term | Meaning | Definition |
| ---- | ------- | ---------- |
| Analyze | To examine the parts of something in detail | - |
| Central Idea | The most important point that an author makes about a topic in a text | - |
| Explicit | Precisely and clearly expressed, leaving no room for doubt | - |
| Implicit | Implied but not stated directly | - |
| Support | Information and evidence used to prove or corroborate something | - |
| Topic | The subject of a text, usually expressed as a single word or phrase in the form of a noun | - |

Read the following excerpt. Then, read each central idea and choose the detail that supports it.
In this excerpt, we learn about the diversity of culture among people isolated by their situation in New Guinea.
From First Contact: New Guinea's Highlanders Encounter the Outside World by Bob Connolly and Robin Anderson
1 The reality turned out to be very different from Trégance's mythic kingdom. There was no unified, hierarchic society. The populations of the highland valleys were splintered into thousands of tiny, isolated enclaves, ranging from a few hundred to a few thousand people. Each enclave had its allies and its enemies, and it was the presence of these enemies that determined the pattern of life and made freedom of movement, and therefore, wide-ranging travel, impossible. Men and women lived out their lives knowing nothing but the familiar environment of their own tribal territory. Travel more than ten miles was difficult and dangerous. Travel from one end of the valley to the other was impossible. Travel beyond the valleys, to the outside world, was inconceivable. There was no outside world.
2 Along with this restriction in movement was a restriction in information. There was no common tongue, but rather a multiplicity of separate languages. This effectively prevented any free flow of information from the outside in, and the inside out. Certainly there were well-established trading routes, extending from both coasts into the highland valleys. Coastal items of value--particularly seashells but also the odd piece of steel-were traded through innumerable hands in innumerable transactions from the coasts into the interior. But by this time information about the origin of the goods was lost. In much the same way, Medieval Europeans enjoyed the exotic products of the Far East without knowing where they came from.

Central Idea: People in New Guinea's highland valleys were splintered into many isolated enclaves.

Detail that supports the central idea: "The populations of the highland valleys were splintered into thousands of tiny, isolated enclaves, ranging from a few hundred to a few thousand people. Each enclave had its allies and its enemies." 

Central Idea: There was a restriction in the movement of people in New Guinea's highland valleys.

Detail that supports the central idea: "Travel more than ten miles was difficult and dangerous. Travel from one end of the valley to the other was impossible. Travel beyond the valleys, to the outside world, was inconceivable." 

Central Idea: There was a restriction in the flow of information in New Guinea's highland valleys.

Detail that supports the central idea: "There was no common tongue, but rather a multiplicity of separate languages. This effectively prevented any free flow of information from the outside in, and the inside out."
